title: Review: Three Weeks With the M2 iPad Air
content:
It's been three weeks since Apple introduced the new iPad Pro and iPad Air models, and after revisiting the iPad Pro last week, we thought we'd take a deeper look at the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå now that it's been out long enough for day-to-day usage testing.

Subscribe to the MacRumors YouTube channel for more videos.

The ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå is the best iPad for people who want a powerful tablet that's also fairly affordable. The M2 chip is close to the same chip that Apple used in the prior-generation ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå, and while it's a little older now, it's still more than powerful enough for anything you might want to do on a tablet.

It is worth noting that Apple recently admitted an error and clarified that the ‚ÄåM2‚Äå chip in the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå has a 9-core GPU instead of a 10-core GPU, but in comparing the ‚ÄåM2‚Äå ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå with 10-core GPU to the ‚ÄåM2‚Äå ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå with 9-core GPU, there was basically no difference in performance for day-to-day tasks and even with more system intensive apps like those used for video editing.

As we said in our M4 ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå review, there's nothing that can really tax the M4 chip, and that's also true of the ‚ÄåM2‚Äå. With iPadOS 18 and the AI capabilities that Apple is working on, chip performance might become much more important in the future, but as of now, the ‚ÄåM2‚Äå ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå isn't going to really struggle with anything you throw at it.

The ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå doesn't have the OLED display technology of the ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå, and it lacks features like ProMotion 120Hz refresh rates, but it otherwise offers a lot of the features you get with the ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå at much more affordable prices. The ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå starts at $999, while the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå starts at $599.

Face ID is not available on the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå, with Apple instead using a Touch ID power button. ‚ÄåTouch ID‚Äå isn't as reliable as ‚ÄåFace ID‚Äå because it can fail depending on finger placement, but it works fine for the most part. The ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå has a Thunderbolt port, but the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå is limited to USB-C, so you can't connect Thunderbolt devices.

You're mostly missing out on OLED and the M4 chip with the ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå, but you also won't be able to use the redesigned Magic Keyboard that Apple made for the ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå because it isn't compatible with the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå. Apple is selling the older Magic Keyboard alongside the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå, and it lacks the aluminum frame and palm rest, function keys, and the larger glass trackpad. You can still use the Apple Pencil Pro and all of its features with the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå, though.

One of the nice features about the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå compared to prior-generation models is the relocated front-facing camera, which is now on the landscape edge instead of the portrait edge. That means the camera is in an upright orientation when it's in landscape mode, such as when connected to a keyboard, so it no longer looks funny if you're FaceTiming while holding the ‚ÄåiPad‚Äå horizontally.

The ‚ÄåM2‚Äå ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå is essentially an ‚ÄåM2‚Äå ‚ÄåiPad Pro‚Äå without the higher-quality display, and it's a good middle tier option if you want solid performance for futureproofing but also don't want to spend $1,000+ on a tablet. For a lot of people who just want a tablet for browsing the web, sending emails, and other simple day-to-day tasks, the ‚ÄåiPad Air‚Äå may even be overkill.
